---
title: "Predictive Modeling and Lineup Optimization in Collegiate Swimming"
subtitle: "A Data-Driven Tool for NCAA Swim Coaches"
author: "Ben Brandt & Andrew Kelley"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [default, metropolis, metropolis-fonts, custom.css]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: "16:9"
---

# The Problem

.left-col[
### Coaches operate with limited analytical infrastructure

At most programs, conference championship preparation looks like this:

- Manually reviewing past meet results in spreadsheets
- Gut-feel decisions on who swims which events
- No systematic way to predict where cut times will fall next season
- Recruiting based on personal network, not roster gap analysis

]

.right-col[
.accent-box[
**The stakes are high.** A single misassigned entry — putting a swimmer in an event 1 place outside scoring — can be the difference between finishing 3rd and 5th as a team.
]

<br>

.highlight-box[
**The question we asked:**

Can we use historical conference meet data and optimization methods to help coaches make better decisions — on lineups, on recruiting, and on goal-setting?
]
]

---

# Our Solution: A Three-Part Analytics Platform

<br>

.metric-row[
.metric-card[
.number[🏊]
.label[**Data Collection**<br>Automated scraping of all NCAA conference championship results from SwimCloud]
]
.metric-card[
.number[📈]
.label[**Predictive Modeling**<br>Linear regression on historical cut times to forecast where the bar will fall next season]
]
.metric-card[
.number[⚙️]
.label[**Lineup Optimization**<br>Integer linear programming to assign swimmers to events that maximize expected team points]
]
]

<br>

.highlight-box[
Delivered as a **Streamlit web application** — no coding required. A coach pastes their SwimCloud team link and gets a full analytics suite in seconds.
]

---
class: section-slide, center, middle

# Part 1: The Data

---
class: balanced

# Data Collection

.left-col[
### Source: SwimCloud.com

SwimCloud is the primary public database for collegiate swim results, including public conference championship results.

**What we scrape:**
- Every event result from every conference championship meet
- Historical data going back as far as available
- Both individual and relay events
- Men's and Women's programs

**Technical approach:**
- `curl_cffi` with Chrome browser impersonation
- Rotating user agents and randomized delays
- Resumable scraping with progress checkpoints
]

.right-col[
```python
# Browser impersonation to avoid detection
session = cffi_requests.Session(
  impersonate="chrome136"
)

# Randomized delay between requests
time.sleep(random.uniform(0.75, 1.5))
```

.highlight-box[
**Data collected per result row:**
- Meet name, year, conference
- Event name, gender, heat type
- Swimmer/team IDs, time, place
]

.badge[conference_id] .badge[meet_name] .badge[event_name] .badge[gender] .badge[time] .badge[place] .badge[swimmer_ID] .badge[team_ID]
]

---
class: balanced

# Data Challenges & Cleaning

.left-col[
### Challenge 1: Conference meet identification

Not every historical meet is the conference championship. Invitationals, dual meets, and ECAC-style events can contaminate the dataset.

**Solution:** Blocklists and championship keyword dictionaries isolate true conference meets.

<br>

### Challenge 2: The "missing year" problem

Some conferences skipped championship years, especially around COVID.

**Solution:** Drop foreign-meet rows from years with no championship.
]

.right-col[
### Challenge 3: Re-seeding

A swimmer from Conference A racing at Conference B's meet must be assigned back to the right conference.

**Solution:** Retain foreign rows only when team membership confirms the match.

<br>

### Challenge 4: Heat type normalization

Finals, prelims, and swimoffs need to be merged into one ordered result list.

```python
FINALS_TYPES = {
  'a final', 'b final', 
  'c final', 'timed finals'
}
```
]

---
class: section-slide, center, middle

# Part 2: Predictive Modeling

---
class: balanced-small

# Predicting Conference Cut Times

### The core question: *What time will you need to score at conference next year?*

.left-col[
**Approach: Linear regression over time**

For each combination of `(conference, event, gender, place)` we fit a regression of `time ~ year` using historical championship results.

Key places modeled: **1st, 8th, 16th, 24th, 32nd**

These correspond to the A Final bubble, B Final bubble, and C Final bubble — the most strategically relevant cutoffs.

**Pre-processing:**
- Rolling 3-year median smoothing to reduce single-year outliers
- Minimum 4 years of data required to fit a model
- Monotonicity enforcement: slower places cannot be predicted faster than faster places
]

.right-col[
```python
fit = stats.linregress(year, time)

predicted_time = (
  fit.intercept + fit.slope * target_year
)

r_squared = fit.rvalue ** 2
```

.highlight-box[
**Output per model:**
- Predicted time for target year
- R² and p-value
- Slope and sample size
]

.accent-box[
In swimming, times generally **get faster each year**. A negative slope reflects the gradual rise in competitive depth.
]
]

---
class: model-output

# Model Output Example

### What the regression produces for a single conference

<br>

| Event | Gender | Place | Predicted Time | R² | p-value | Slope | n |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 100 Free | M | 1 | 42.81 | 0.91 | 0.001 | -0.13 | 8 |
| 100 Free | M | 8 | 44.12 | 0.88 | 0.002 | -0.10 | 8 |
| 100 Free | M | 16 | 45.63 | 0.82 | 0.005 | -0.08 | 7 |
| 200 IM | F | 1 | 1:54.30 | 0.86 | 0.004 | -0.31 | 6 |
| 200 IM | F | 8 | 1:57.44 | 0.79 | 0.011 | -0.24 | 6 |

<br>

.highlight-box[
These predicted cuts are the backbone of every downstream feature — the optimizer, the cut curve visualizations, the recruit filter, and the roster gap analysis all consume this output.
]

.footnote-custom[*Example values shown for illustration. Actual outputs vary by conference.*]

---
class: section-slide, center, middle

# Part 3: The Application

---

# Application Architecture

<br>

.flowchart[
``` 
SwimCloud (web)
      │
      ▼
swimcloud_scraper.py ──► conference_X_results.csv (all conferences)
      │
      ▼
reseed_conference_results.py ──► conference_X_results_reseeded.csv
      │
      ▼
conference_cut_regression.py ──► regression_outputs.csv
                                        │
                          ┌─────────────┼──────────────┐
                          ▼             ▼              ▼
                    Home Page    Lineup Optimizer   Cut Analysis
                  (live roster    (PuLP ILP)       + Recruit Finder
                   via API)
```
]

.highlight-box[
The data pipeline runs **offline** (scraping + modeling). The Streamlit app is **live** — it pulls a team's current season times from SwimCloud's API in real time when a coach enters their team link.
]

---
class: home-slide

# Page 1: Home & Roster Loading

.left-col[
### How a coach gets started

1. Navigate to their team page on SwimCloud
2. Paste the URL into the app
3. The app calls SwimCloud's API to pull **current-season best times** for every swimmer on the roster across all 13 individual yard events
4. Data is stored in session state — all three downstream pages consume it without re-scraping

.highlight-box[
Live API access means a coach can use the tool **mid-season** — times update as swimmers race.
]
]

.right-col[
### What loads automatically

| Event | Swimmer | Time |
|---|---|---|
| 100 Free | J. Smith | 43.21 |
| 100 Free | M. Jones | 44.05 |
| 200 Back | A. Lee | 1:47.33 |
| 200 IM | T. Brown | 1:53.90 |
| … | … | … |

Both **Men's and Women's** rosters load simultaneously. All 13 individual yard events are captured.

.badge[50 Free] .badge[100 Free] .badge[200 Free] .badge[500 Free] .badge[1650 Free] .badge[100 Back] .badge[200 Back] .badge[100 Breast] .badge[200 Breast] .badge[100 Fly] .badge[200 Fly] .badge[200 IM] .badge[400 IM]
]

---

# Page 2: Conference Lineup Optimizer

.left-col[
### The optimization problem

**Decision variables:** Binary — does swimmer *s* swim event *e*? (1 or 0)

**Objective:** Maximize total expected team points

**Constraints:**
- Each swimmer entered in ≤ N individual events (typically 3)
- Total roster entries ≤ max cap (typically 18 swimmers)
- A swimmer can only be entered in events they have a time for
- User can manually exclude swimmers (injured, not attending)

**Solver:** PuLP with CBC backend

]

.right-col[
### How expected points are calculated

For each (swimmer, event) pair:

1. Pull their season best time
2. Use the regression cut table to **interpolate their estimated finishing place**
3. Map estimated place → expected points using the scoring table
4. Swimmers near the scoring bubble (within N places) are flagged but still included

.accent-box[
**Why ILP?** A greedy approach fails — the same swimmer appearing in 3 events creates interdependencies. ILP finds the globally optimal assignment.
]
]

---

# Page 3: Cut Analysis

### Six visualizations to understand your team's conference position

.feature-grid[
.feature-card[
**Cut Time Curve**
<p>Predicted time vs. place for any event, with A/B/C Final lines visible.</p>
]
.feature-card[
**Time-to-Cut Waterfall**
<p>Each swimmer plotted against the cut curve to show scorers and bubble athletes.</p>
]
.feature-card[
**Event Versatility**
<p>Highlights swimmers who project to score in multiple events.</p>
]
.feature-card[
**Projected Points Trend**
<p>Tracks how team scoring power changes year over year.</p>
]
.feature-card[
**Scoring Depth**
<p>Counts swimmers by A Final, B Final, C Final, near scorer, and off pace zones.</p>
]
.feature-card[
**Recruiting Gaps**
<p>Breaks projected points down by stroke group and event to guide recruiting.</p>
]
]

---

# Page 4: Recruit Finder

.left-col[
### Finding the right prospects

The tool ships with a pre-scraped database of uncommitted recruits from SwimCloud's recruiting rankings, including each swimmer's top 5 events and times.

**Filters available:**
- Gender & graduation year
- Power Index range (fastest ↔ slowest)
- Desired event
- **Conference scoring window filter** — only show recruits whose time falls between the projected 1st and last scoring place (with margin)
- **Roster fit flag** — automatically identifies recruits whose events fill the team's *N* weakest scoring events
]

.right-col[
.highlight-box[
**Example output row:**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Name | Emma R. |
| Grad Year | 2028 |
| Power Index | 3.42 |
| Roster Fit | 200 Back, 400 IM |
| Event 1 | 200 Back (SCY) — 1:56.4 |
| Event 2 | 400 IM (SCY) — 4:16.2 |
| Event 3 | 200 IM (SCY) — 2:01.8 |
]

.accent-box[
The "Roster Fit" column is automatically computed by ranking the team's current events by projected points and flagging recruits who fill the bottom N events.
]
]

---
class: section-slide, center, middle

# Implications & Ethics

---
class: balanced-large

# Implications for Stakeholders

.left-col[
### For Coaches
- **Lineup decisions backed by data**, not just intuition
- **Recruiting clarity** around exact event/time targets
- **Honest goal-setting** from predicted cut trends

### For Athletic Departments
- Low-cost analytics capability without hiring a dedicated analyst
- Objective framework for evaluating program trajectory
]

.right-col[
### For Swimmers
- Better event selection based on where they are genuinely competitive
- Transparent expectations from coaches

### For the Broader Ecosystem
- Built to cover **all NCAA conferences**
- Gives mid-major programs access to practical analytics

.highlight-box[
The tool is descriptive and advisory — it surfaces information. Final decisions remain entirely with the coach.
]
]

---
class: balanced-large

# Ethical, Legal & Societal Considerations

.left-col[
### Data & Privacy
- Data scraped is **publicly available** on SwimCloud
- Swimmer IDs and names are used only for roster matching
- Recruit data includes public rankings only; no contact info

### Scraping Ethics
- The scraper uses rate limiting, randomized delays, and session management to avoid server strain
- No authentication bypass or paywall circumvention — only public endpoints
]

.right-col[
### Fairness & Bias
- The model is only as good as the historical data
- Conferences with fewer years of data should be interpreted with more caution
- Consistent improvements in a conference (e.g., recruiting a dominant program) may not be captured well by a linear model

### Over-reliance Risk
.accent-box[
**Important caveat:** Outputs are projections based on historical averages. Race-day conditions, taper timing, and swimmer development are not modeled.
]
]

---

# Current Status & Next Steps

.left-col[
### What's working now
- ✅ Full scraping pipeline operational
- ✅ Regression model producing outputs
- ✅ Streamlit app functional across all 4 pages
- ✅ Recruit database populated for 2027 & 2028 classes
- ✅ Bot-detection evasion and resumable scraping

### Data status
- 🔄 Actively scraping all NCAA conferences
- Conferences vary in data richness — larger conferences with longer championship histories produce the most reliable models
]

.right-col[
### Planned improvements
- **Relay optimizer** — include relay event point projections in the ILP
- **Multi-year recruit tracking** — flag recruits who have been uncommitted across multiple seasons
- **Conference comparison** — let coaches benchmark their conference's cut trends against others
- **Confidence-weighted scoring** — weight expected points by the model's R² to penalize low-confidence predictions in the optimizer

.highlight-box[
The end goal is a **freely available resource** for coaches at any NCAA program — giving smaller programs analytical capabilities that have historically only existed at well-funded departments.
]
]

---

class: section-slide, center, middle

# Thank You


Questions welcome.
